Call for proposals: Transforming Mental Health in Higher Education

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Dr. Thaddeus Mantaro
6mo
  • Report this post

Are you hoping to make a difference in the the landscape of mental health and higher education?! Ryan Travia, Ed.D., FACHA, Marcus Hotaling and I are excited to share the call for proposals for a new NASPA publication, "Transforming Mental Health in Higher Education: Leadership Strategies and Innovative Approaches". We invite chapter proposals for a forthcoming edited book examining leadership strategies and innovative approaches to mental health service delivery in higher education, with a particular focus on post-pandemic transformations in counseling center operations, institutional mental health frameworks, and community-based approaches to student well-being. This comprehensive volume will analyze contemporary challenges including mental health policy implications, evidence-based treatment modalities, crisis intervention protocols, resource allocation strategies, and the pedagogical integration of faculty as mental health allies within institutional ecosystems. We seek empirically grounded contributions from counseling center directors, student affairs practitioners, faculty scholars, and clinical researchers who can provide theoretical frameworks, methodological innovations, and applied research findings that advance our understanding of contemporary collegiate mental health service delivery. The book will synthesize scholarship with practitioner wisdom through various sections, while examining institutional variability across sectors (public, private, community colleges, etc.). We are committed to centering diverse scholarly voices and identities to ensure representation in this essential resource for mental health research, policy, and practice in higher education. Please note that authors of accepted proposals may be paired with a co-author(s) to ensure the broadest representation possible and are subject to final approval by NASPA. For more information about the chapters and specific details, please see the following: https://lnkd.in/e2MrBnjA. Note that if you are not a current NASPA member, you will be prompted to create a free account.

  • European Down Syndrome Association

    110 followers

    7mo
    • Report this post

    Inclusive education doesn’t just improve learning, it improves lives. Across Europe, we’re increasingly seeing what that when health and education systems work together, outcomes for people with Down syndrome multiply. 🔹 The DSi Education Guidelines remind us that learning is a lifelong right and that access to quality, inclusive education promotes independence, communication, and community participation. 🔹 The NDSS Mental Health report shows that at least half of all people with Down syndrome will face a significant mental health concern, often linked to undiagnosed hearing, vision, or sleep issues. 🔹 When schools coordinate with families, medical teams, and therapists, they create environments where students are not just present, but thriving — academically, socially, and emotionally. This integration is more than good practice, it’s a public health strategy. Healthy learners are better learners. And inclusive classrooms are healthier communities. Health inclusion and educational inclusion must advance together not seperately. 📘 Learn more: International Guidelines for the Education of Learners with Down Syndrome (Down Syndrome International) Mental Health & Down Syndrome (National Down Syndrome Society) #InclusiveEducation #PublicHealth #DownSyndrome #LeadershipInInclusion #EDSA #EducationForAll

  • Perry Blankenship Psy D DMin EdS NCSP LPC
    6mo
    • Report this post

    Expanding “Grow Your Own” Beyond Teachers: A Call to Strengthen the School Mental Health Pipeline Across the country, “Grow Your Own Teacher” initiatives have successfully opened pathways for students to enter the education profession—especially in rural communities. These programs are forward-thinking, equitable, and community-centered. But our workforce needs go much deeper. To truly support students, we must expand these initiatives to include school psychologists, school counselors, social workers, and licensed professional counselors. The national shortage is real and well-documented: High caseloads Limited access to specialized services Burnout across mental health roles Increased student needs in trauma, behavior, SEL, and family support It’s time to build intentional pipelines—starting in high school. Imagine vocational and career-technical programs that offer: Early exposure to counseling and psychology careers Coursework in human development, behavioral health, and crisis response Partnerships with school districts, universities, and licensure boards Mentorship and shadowing opportunities Tuition-supported pathways to help students return to serve their own communities “Growing our own” shouldn’t be limited to teachers. It should include the entire ecosystem of professionals who support student mental health and well-being. If school systems want long-term sustainability and a resilient workforce, expanding this model is not just beneficial—it’s essential. I welcome conversations with districts, policymakers, and community partners who are interested in building the next generation of school mental health professionals. —Perry Blankenship, Psy.D., D.Min., Ed.S., LPC, ALPS

  • The Mental Health Literacy Collaborative

    5,989 followers

    7mo

    We are excited to share the recent launch of NAMI’s interactive mental health education map that recently launched. We are so grateful for the opportunity to collaborate with the amazing team at NAMI National on this project. Special shoutout to Nicole Kennedy for her incredible research contributions. On the new webpage, NAMI says, “Many students learn about general health issues in health education programs, but those curricula have not always traditionally included mental health. More recently, there has been momentum behind the availability of curricula focused on mental health, which can increase students’ awareness of the signs of mental health conditions in themselves or others and how to seek help...NAMI’s School Mental Health Education Map provides a state landscape of mental health education requirements in schools.” This interactive platform allows viewers to review state policies related to mental health education. There is also a “Supporting Policies” tab for more information related to the state policies as well as an option to connect with the NAMI National state affairs team if viewers believe something may be missing. This website is an excellent tool for people engaged in mental health education to visit regularly. It offers viewers a snapshot of the great policy work being done across the country to ensure young people receive effective mental health education in schools. Congratulations, NAMI, for this amazing upstream resource! Check out the map here https://lnkd.in/e5hBB2jx #mentalhealtheducation #schoolmentalhealth #upstream #collaboration

    Here's a peek into some of the work I've been doing with Wellstream/Bienamont for the past several months: a pan-Canadian standard for substance use prevention, education, and intervention in K-12 schools. The draft standard is available for public review. Read more below and share widely!

    Wellstream / Bienamont

    98 followers

    7mo

    We’re proud to share that the draft standard (PRV STD-16:2025), 𝘚𝘤𝘩𝘰𝘰𝘭-𝘉𝘢𝘴𝘦𝘥 𝘚𝘶𝘣𝘴𝘵𝘢𝘯𝘤𝘦 𝘜𝘴𝘦 𝘗𝘳𝘦𝘷𝘦𝘯𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯, 𝘌𝘥𝘶𝘤𝘢𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯, 𝘢𝘯𝘥 𝘐𝘯𝘵𝘦𝘳𝘷𝘦𝘯𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯: 𝘈 𝘔𝘶𝘭𝘵𝘪-𝘛𝘪𝘦𝘳𝘦𝘥 𝘢𝘯𝘥 𝘋𝘦𝘷𝘦𝘭𝘰𝘱𝘮𝘦𝘯𝘵𝘢𝘭 𝘈𝘱𝘱𝘳𝘰𝘢𝘤𝘩 𝘧𝘰𝘳 𝘒𝘪𝘯𝘥𝘦𝘳𝘨𝘢𝘳𝘵𝘦𝘯 𝘵𝘰 𝘎𝘳𝘢𝘥𝘦 12 𝘚𝘤𝘩𝘰𝘰𝘭𝘴, is now available for public review — in both English and French! Developed through a partnership between Wellstream/Bienamont, the Canadian Association of School System Administrators (CASSA-ACGCS), and the Canadian Centre on Substance Use and Addiction (CCSA), and managed by CSA Group, this landmark standard represents months of collaborative work with youth, educators, health professionals, and researchers from across Canada. Grounded in research and practice-based insights, the standard offers guidance to help K–12 schools develop comprehensive approaches to substance use prevention, education, and intervention. 💬 𝐘𝐨𝐮𝐫 𝐈𝐧𝐩𝐮𝐭 𝐌𝐚𝐭𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐬 We invite you to review the draft and share your feedback — your insights will help ensure the final standard truly reflects the diverse realities of Canadian schools. 📅 Public Review Period: November 6 – December 6, 2025 🌐 Available in both English and French 📝 Access the draft and submit comments through the CSA platform: 🔗 English https://lnkd.in/encGYbUa 🔗 French https://lnkd.in/eCUabxbp All feedback received during the review period will be carefully considered by our Executive Working Group and Expert Advisory Committee as we prepare for final release in early 2026. Let’s work together to strengthen school-based approaches to student well-being across Canada. Please share this post to help spread the word! 🌍

    School-Based Substance Use Prevention, Education, and Intervention: A Multi-Tiered and Developmental Approach for Kindergarten to Grade 12 Schools (Private Standard) publicreview.csa.ca

    The Campus Mental Health Action Tracker is a free bi-lingual tool designed to help post-secondary institutions strengthen student well-being. The Tracker supports self-assessment, documentation, and continuous improvement. It was developed by SRDC in partnership with the Mental Health Commission of Canada (https://hubs.la/Q03Pk4y90 https://hubs.la/Q03Pk4z70) and campuses nationwide with funding from the Rossy Foundation to support implementation of the National Standard of Canada for Mental Health and Well-Being for Post-Secondary Students.
